title: Greece Civilization_The Histories (By Herodotus)
paragraphs: If a new translation of Herodotus does not justify itself, it is hardly justifiable to include it in a preface. The question of whether such a translation is necessary can be left for consideration elsewhere. As it seemed that even after Stein's recollation of the Medicean manuscript. There were doubts felt by some scholars regarding the true reading in certain places of this manuscript. The translation of the Bible into English has been a subject of much debate among scholars. It is widely acknowledged that the King James Version (KJV) is one of the most influential translations, but I believed it was essential to investigate it myself in areas where questions about translation arise, particularly in nearly 500 passages; and the findings, when significant, are documented in the notes. No consistent system has been adopted, and the result will therefore be open to criticism in many details. The aim has been to avoid altering the form of those names that are fairly established in the English language of literature, while preserving their traditional forms. This approach avoids pedantry by not making significant changes to well-established names. At the same time, it seeks to correct the orthography of names that are not as widely recognized, looking instead to Greek rather than Latin for guidance.

Woods  After this, certain Greeks arrived in the city of Tyre in Phoenicia and abducted King Europa's daughter. It is likely that these individuals were from Crete. They had finally received their revenge for a previous injury.

Alexander, the son of King Priam, sought a wife through force after hearing about these events. He intended to take a wife from Hellas, confident that he would not have to provide compensation for his actions, since the Greeks had offered none in return. Up to this point, it is said that nothing more happened than the carrying away of women on both sides. However, after this, the Hellenes were greatly to blame. They set the first example of war by making an expedition into Asia before the Barbarians made any into Europe. Croesus, the first of the known barbarians, conquered some of the Greeks and compelled them to pay tribute. He also won over others, making them his allies.

I will place you in the room where we sleep, behind the open door. After I have entered, my wife will also come to lie down. He then, since he might not avoid it, gave consent. Candaules, considering it was time to rest, led Gyges to the chamber. Straightaway after this, the woman appeared. Gyges looked upon her as she entered and as she laid down her garments. When she had turned her back towards him, as she went to the bed, he slipped away from his hiding place and began to leave. As soon as day broke, she remained silent. She did not make any outward gesture, but once morning arrived, she summoned the servants who were closest to her and instructed them to bring Gyges to her presence. When Gyges arrived, the woman said to him, "There are now two paths available to you, Gyges. I offer you a choice between them; which one will you prefer?"

As night fell, Gyges found himself trapped with no escape from his fate. He had to choose between being killed or killing Candaules. The woman led him to the bedchamber and handed him a dagger, hiding him behind the very door they stood in front of. Then afterwards, while Candaules was sleeping, Gyges came up to him secretly and slew him. He obtained both his wife and his kingdom. Archilochos the Parian, who lived at that time, mentioned this event in a trimeter iambic verse. 11 13 He obtained the kingdom, however, and was strengthened in it by the Oracle at Delphi. For when the Lydians were angry over the fate of Candaules and had risen in arms, a treaty was made between Gyges' followers and the other Lydians: if the Oracle declared him king of the Lydians, he would be king; otherwise, he would return power to the sons of Heracles. The Oracle gave an answer, and Gyges became king accordingly. The Pythian prophetess also said that vengeance would come upon the descendants of Gyges in the fifth generation.

The gold and silver dedicated by Gyges was named Gygian by the people of Delphi, in honor of the one who had made the offering. Now Gyges, as soon as he became king, led an army against Miletos and Smyrna. He took the lower town of Colophon. His reign lasted eight years. There is no record of any other significant deeds during his time in power. I will now speak about Ardys, the son of Gyges, who succeeded him as king. He conquered Priene and launched an invasion against Miletus. While he ruled Sardis, the Kimmerians, who had been forced out of their homeland by the nomadic Scythians, arrived in Asia and captured Sardis, leaving only the citadel intact. 16

When Ardys had ruled for forty-nine years, Sadyattes his son succeeded him and reigned for twelve years. After him came Alyattes. He did not return as he had hoped, but instead arrived with significant losses. During his reign, he accomplished many deeds that were worthy of note. Note: The original text was likely scanned from a printed source, which may have introduced errors such as the OCR scanning error in the number "17". For six of the eleven years, Sadyattes, the son of Ardys, remained ruler of the Lydians. He was the same one who had invaded the land of Miletos at the times mentioned. In fact, Sadyattes was the first to initiate the war. However, for the five years that followed these initial six, the war continued under Alyattes, the son of Sadyattes, who inherited it from his father and worked diligently on it.

Alyattes fell ill after the army's return to Sardis. His prolonged illness led him to send messengers to consult the Oracle at Delphi, either on the advice of someone else or out of a desire to seek divine guidance regarding his condition. When the Greeks arrived at Delphi, the Pythian prophetess declared that she would not provide an answer until they rebuilt the Temple of Athena, which they had destroyed at Assessos in the region of Miletus. 20 This Thrasylus did and thus proclaimed to the end that a herald from Sardis, seeing a vast quantity of provisions carelessly piled up, and the people feasting, might report this to Alyattes. And so it happened; for when the herald returned to Sardis after seeing this and delivering to Thrasylus the charge given to him by the king of Lydia, the peace which was made came about, as I am informed, merely because of this.

After the peace was established, they became guest-friends and allies. Alyattes then built two temples to Athena at Assuros, replacing one he had previously constructed, and he recovered from his illness. Arion, it is said, spent most of his time with Periander. He developed a desire to travel to Italy and Sicily. After acquiring significant wealth in these places, he wanted to return to Corinth. He set forth therefore from Taras, and as he had faith in the Corinthians more than in any other man, he hired a ship with a crew of Corinthians.

He did not prevail upon them, but the men who were carrying him told him to either kill himself there so he could receive a proper burial, or jump straight into the sea. Arion requested that they permit him to stand on the deck, dressed as a minstrel, while singing. He vowed to end his life afterward. He had arrived, and then he told everything that had happened. Periander was uncertain about his story, so he kept Arion on guard and wouldn't let him leave anywhere, while keeping a close eye on the people who had brought him there. When these messengers arrived, he asked them about any news regarding Arion. They reported that he was safe in Italy and had been left at Taras, where he was faring well. Suddenly, Arion appeared before them, still in the same form as when he leapt from the ship. The messengers were amazed, and their initial denial was no longer tenable when questioned.

Alyattes, king of Lydia, waged war against the Milesians after which he died, having reigned for 57 years. Upon his death, Croesus, his son, succeeded him at the age of 35 and went on to fight against the Hellenes, starting with the Ephesians.

When Solon had moved Croesus to inquire further about the story of Tellos, recounting how many points of happiness he had, the king asked again whom he had seen proper to be placed next after this man, supposing that he himself would certainly obtain at least the second place. But he replied: "Cleobis and Biton: for these two brothers, from Argos by birth, possessed a sufficiency of wealth and, in addition, strength of body such as I shall describe."  Solon ranked Athens second in terms of happiness. Croesus became enraged and said, "Athenian guest, have you abandoned our thriving state for naught, preferring even the humblest citizens over us?" "Croesus, you are seeking knowledge about human fortunes. I am aware that the gods are jealous and prone to disrupting our lives."

For many wealthy men, happiness eludes them, while those with moderate means are fortunate. In reality, a rich man who is unhappy has only two advantages over the poor man who is fortunate; in contrast, the latter has many more than the former. After Solon had departed, a great retribution from God came upon Croesus, who was likely punished for judging himself to be the happiest man. Croesus had two sons. One son was severely impaired, being both deaf and mute. The other son excelled in all aspects, surpassing his peers of the same age. His name was Atys.

A man from Sardis arrived at the city, a Phrygian by birth and of noble lineage, but with hands stained by misfortune. In Olympos, in the region of Mysia, a massive boar was said to roam. He begged the king either to let him hunt it or to persuade him that his fate was better as it stood.

Croesus replied, "My son, I am not motivated by any perception of cowardice or unbecoming qualities in you. A dream appeared to me during my sleep, warning that your life would be brief and that you would meet an untimely end at the hands of a spear-point." The young man replied: "It may be forgiven that you had concern for me after witnessing such a vision. However, what you do not comprehend, and the meaning of which has eluded you, is my duty to explain."

If the dream had told you that I should end my life by a tusk, or anything resembling that, it would be right for you to do as you are doing. But it said "by a spear point." Since we cannot determine the original context of the provided text, I will return nothing. " 40 "My son, you have partially won me over by presenting your argument about the dream. As a result, I have changed my mind and granted you permission to pursue the hunt." " 41 Having thus said, Croesus went to summon Adrastos, the Phrygian. When he arrived, he addressed him thus: "Adrastos, when you were struck by a grievous misfortune (of which I do not reproach you), I cleansed you and have received you into my house, supplying all your expenses."

Now therefore, since I have shown you kindness, you are obligated to repay me with kindness. I ask that you protect my son who is going on a hunt, lest any evil robbers harm him on his journey. Additionally, you should also seek out opportunities where your deeds will bring you fame, for it is your birthright to do so. You possess the strength and ability to achieve this. " 42  " 43 When he had replied to Croesus in words like these, they subsequently set forth, accompanied by chosen young men and dogs.

And being grievously troubled by the misfortune, he called upon Zeus, the god of cleansing, to protest against what he had suffered at the hands of his guest. He also invoked the Protector of Suppliants and the Guardian of Friendship, naming the same god twice, but using different titles. This was because when he received the guest into his house, he had unknowingly harbored the slayer of his son. Furthermore, having sent him as a protector, he had found him to be the worst of enemies. 45 After this, the Lydians arrived with the body, followed by its slayer. The latter took his position before the corpse and surrendered to Croesus, holding out his hands and asking the king to kill him over the body. He spoke of his past misfortune and said that in addition to this, he had now become the killer of the man who had cleansed him of it; and that for him, life was no longer worth living. For two years, Croesus remained quiet in his mourning, having lost his son. However, after this period, the overthrow of Astyages' rule by Cyrus, the son of Cambyses, and the growing power of the Persians, lifted Croesus from his grief and prompted him to take action against the Persians, if possible, before their power reached its peak.

And when the rest who had been sent round arrived, they brought with them the answers of the Oracles. Croesus then unfolded the writings one by one and examined them. At first, none pleased him, but when he heard that the Oracle at Delphi was from there, he immediately worshiped the god and accepted the answer. He judged 42 to be a reliable number because it was the only one that matched his own actions. This was the answer given to Croesus from Delphi; and as regards the answer of Amphiaraos, I cannot tell what he replied to the Lydians after they had performed their customary rituals in his temple. 50 He then considered inquiring about the most influential individuals among the Hellenes whom he could win over as allies.

If, therefore, the entire Pelasgian race were like this, then the Attic race, being Pelasgian, would have lost its language at the same time it underwent the change to become Hellenic. The Hellenic race has used the same language since its inception. I clearly perceive this, as it has remained consistent throughout its history. However, after parting ways with the Pelasgian race at first, it began to expand from a small beginning, eventually giving rise to the great number of races we observe today, largely due to the addition of many Barbarian races.

As soon as he had made the offering, the cauldrons that stood on the hearth, filled with pieces of flesh and water, began to boil without any visible flame and overflowed. When Chilon had thus recommended, Hippocrates was not willing to be persuaded. As a result, Peisistratos was born to him later. Peisistratos, when the Athenians from the shore were at odds with those from the plain, led by Megacles, son of Alcmaion, and Lycurgos, son of Aristolaïdes, from the plain's side, sought power for himself and gathered a third faction. After collecting supporters and proclaiming himself leader of the men of the mountain-lands, he devised a plan as follows: He inflicted wounds on himself and his mules, then drove his chariot into the market-place, as if he had just escaped from his opponents, who, according to him, sought to kill him when he entered the country. He asked the commons for protection, citing his reputation in command against the Megarians, during which he captured Nisaia and performed other notable services.

The followers of Megacles and those of Lycurgos soon joined forces and drove him out. Those who had driven out Peisistratus later found themselves at odds with each other once more. So, having received back the despotism in the manner described, Peisistratos married the daughter of Megacles, as agreed upon. However, since he already had young sons and the descendants of Alcmaeon were said to be under a curse, 73 he did not wish any more children to be born to him from his newly-married wife.

The woman initially concealed the information, but eventually shared it with her mother. The mother then informed her husband, Megacles. After a ten-year absence, they returned to Eretria. Their first stop in Attica was Marathon. While they camped here, supporters from the city arrived, along with others who had fled from the various districts, where despotic rule was more appealing than freedom.

The Athenians in the city paid little attention to the gathering while Peisistratus was collecting funds. However, once he seized control of Marathon, their interest shifted. It wasn't until they learned that he was marching towards the city that they took action to counter his move. The oracle spoke to him, inspired by the gods. Peisistratos, understanding the message, acknowledged the prophecy and led his army against the enemy. The Athenians, still in the city, were engaged in their morning meal when some of them, after eating, spent their time playing dice or sleeping. Meanwhile, the forces of Peisistratus attacked the Athenians, forcing them to flee.

He mounted his sons on horseback and sent them ahead. They overtook the fleeing people and told them what Peisistratus had ordered: "Be at peace and go back to your own homes." 64 Croesus heard that the Athenians were facing a difficult situation, but when it came to the Lacedemonians, he learned that they had narrowly escaped great hardships and had recently emerged victorious over the Tegeans in their conflict. Lycurgos, a man of great renown from Sparta, visited the Oracle at Delphi. Upon entering the temple's sanctuary, the Pythian prophetess greeted him with the following words: "You have arrived, Lycurgos, in this sacred shrine, favored by Zeus and revered by all who dwell on Mount Olympus."

For as soon as he became guardian, he changed all the prevailing laws and took measures so they would not transgress his institutions. Lycurgos then established what pertained to war, including Enomoties, Triecads, and Common Meals. He also added the Ephors and the Senate. The Spartans had good laws. After Lycurgos' death, they built a temple in his honor and paid him great respect. It does not contain any obvious errors that would require correction.

Now, the eldest citizens among the "Well-doers" replace those who have passed from the ranks of the "Horsemen" every five years. During the year they leave the "Horsemen," they are allowed to travel freely to various locations within the Spartan State without interruption. 68 He had thus conjectured that he would return to Sparta, whereupon he informed the Lacedemonians of the entire situation. They then brought a false accusation against him and banished him from their city. So, having come to Tegea, he told the smith of his evil fortune and endeavoured to hire from him the enclosure. However, at first, the smith would not allow him to have it. Eventually, Lichas persuaded him, and he took up residence there. He then dug up the grave, gathered together the bones, and went with them away to Sparta. The Lacedemonians accepted the alliance because he chose them as his friends, preferring them above all the other Hellenes.

For the boundary between the Median empire and Lydia was the river Halys. It flows from the mountain-land of Armenia through the Kilikians and then has the Matienians on its right-hand side and the Phrygians on its left-hand side. As it continues to flow, it passes by these groups and bounds the Cappadocian Syrians on one side and the Paphlagonians on the other. Croesus marched into Cappadocia for two main reasons. Firstly, he sought to expand his territory by acquiring the land, in addition to his existing possessions. Secondly, he was motivated by a desire for revenge against Cyrus, whom he believed had wronged him through the actions of Astyages. After this, seeing that Alyattes would not give up the Scythians when Kyaxares demanded them, a war broke out between the Lydians and the Medes, lasting five years. During these years, the Medes frequently defeated the Lydians, while the Lydians often repelled the Medes. They also fought a battle by night: in 88. As they continued to wage war with fortunes evenly matched, a battle took place in the sixth year, during which it happened that when the fight began, the day suddenly turned into night. The Lydians, however, and the Medes, seeing that it had become night instead of day, immediately stopped fighting and became even more eager to make peace between them.

Cyrus, the son of Astyages, who was his mother's father, had conquered this land. He had taken its ruler into captivity for a specific reason that would be revealed later in the historical account that followed. This then was the complaint which Croesus had against Cyrus when he sent to the Oracles to ask if he should march against the Persians. A deceitful answer had come back to him, and so he marched into the dominion of the Persians, supposing that the answer was favourable to himself. He captured the city of the Pterians and sold its inhabitants into slavery. He also conquered the surrounding towns and forced the Syrians, who had committed no wrongdoing, to leave their homes. Cyrus, having gathered his own forces, came to meet Croesus with an army that included everyone living in the surrounding area. Before he began to lead his army, he had sent heralds to the Ionians and tried to persuade them to revolt against Croesus. However, the Ionians refused to comply with his request.

When Cyrus arrived and set up camp near Croesus, they engaged each other in combat in the land of Pteria. After a fierce battle, with many casualties on both sides, they eventually parted ways at night, neither having gained the upper hand. 77 As soon as he arrived in Sardis, he sent heralds to his various allies to inform them that they should assemble at Sardis by the fifth month from that time. However, he immediately disbanded the army he had with him, which consisted mainly of mercenary troops, and let it go without a fight. This decision was unexpected, given that Cyrus had previously fought against him with equal success, but ultimately decided to march on Sardis anyway. 78 The Telmessians, however, made their decision thus: that an army speaking a foreign tongue should be sought by Croesus to invade his land, and that this would subdue the native inhabitants; for they said that the serpent was born of the soil, while the horse was an enemy and a stranger.

The men of Telmessos replied to Croesus after he had been captured, unaware of the events that had occurred at Sardis and to Croesus himself. 79 Cyrus, however, soon as Croesus marched away after the battle fought in Pteria, having learned that Croesus meant to disband his army after he had marched away, took counsel with himself and concluded it was good for him to march as quickly as possible to Sardis before the power of the Lydians gathered again. Croesus, despite being in a dire situation, continued to lead the Lydians into battle. Such was his charge; he set the camels opposite the horsemen. He did this because the horse has a fear of the camel and cannot endure either to see its form or to scent its smell. For this reason, the trick had been devised: it was designed to make the cavalry of Croesus useless, for that very force with which the Lydian king expected to shine most.

The Lydians, however, did not behave like cowards. When they realized what was about to happen, they dismounted their horses and engaged the Persians in a fierce battle on foot. The Lydians, after a prolonged period, fled in large numbers from both sides. Driven back into their fortified city, they were subsequently besieged by the Persians. 81 For the former messengers, who had been sent around to give notice, were now instructed to assemble at Sardis by the fifth month. However, these messengers were being sent out to request their assistance as quickly as possible, for Croesus was under siege. 82

When the Argives arrived to rescue their territory from being cut off, both sides agreed to a truce. They decided that 300 soldiers from each side would engage in battle, and the nation with the better outcome would claim the disputed land. Additionally, they agreed that the main forces of each army would withdraw to their own countries, rather than staying on the battlefield, for fear that if one side saw its comrades suffering defeat, it might rush to their aid. The two men from Argos, assuming they were the winners, set off towards Argos. However, the Lacedaemonian Othryades, after stripping the corpses of the Argives and taking their arms to his own camp, stayed put. On the next day, both sides arrived to inquire about the outcome. For some time, each side claimed victory for themselves. The one side argued that more of their men remained alive, while the other side stated that theirs had fled and the enemy's corpses lay scattered. However, as they disputed this, they eventually turned on each other and began fighting. After many casualties on both sides, the Lacedemonians emerged victorious.

The Lacedemonians, however, adopted a different law, which stated that they would wear their hair long from that point on. Prior to this, they did not have long hair. Now, when the fourteenth day arrived after Croesus had begun to be besieged, Cyrus proclaimed to his army and sent horsemen around its various sections, announcing that he would reward the man who was the first to climb over the wall. After this, the army made a final attempt. When it failed, all remaining forces ceased their attack. A Mardian named Hyroiades then attempted to approach the citadel on the side where no guard had been posted. They had no fear that the citadel would be taken from that side, as it was steep and unassailable.

He led the charge, followed by other Persian warriors, who gradually closed in on the city. Soon, Sardis fell to their forces, and the entire city was laid waste. 85 Croesus himself experienced this event in the following way: He had a son, whom I previously mentioned, who possessed a good temperament but lacked the ability to speak. In his former time of prosperity, Croesus had done everything that was possible for him. He had devised other things as well as sending messengers to Delphi to inquire about himself. The Persians then took possession of Sardis and captured King Croesus, who had ruled for fourteen years, after being besieged for fourteen days. He had fulfilled the prophecy by bringing an end to his vast empire.

They say he did this. But as Croesus stood on the pyre, a memory of Solon's words came to him, even though he was in a terrible state. Solon had once said that no one who lived could truly be called happy. While Croesus discussed these matters, the pyre had already been lit, and its edges were burning. He wept before the god, and soon, dark clouds assembled under a clear sky, bringing forth a tempest that rained violently. The funeral pyre was extinguished.

Cyrus, having perceived that Croesus was a lover of the gods and a good man, caused him to be brought down from the pyre. He then asked Croesus: "Croesus, tell me who among all men persuaded you to march on my land, making you an enemy instead of a friend." "I told you, O king, that I had done this for your sake and at my own expense. It was the god of the Greeks who goaded me into leading my army." Cyrus was concerned about what Croesus had said. He asked everyone else to leave and then turned to Croesus and asked, "What do you see that would benefit me in this situation?" Croesus replied, "Since the gods have given you to me as a gift, I think it's only right if I share any additional insight with you." The Persians, who are by nature unruly, lack wealth. If therefore you suffer them to carry off in plunder great wealth and take possession of it, then it is to be looked for that you will experience this result. You must expect that whoever gets the largest share will make an insurrection against you.

Now therefore, if what I say pleases you, do this: Set spearmen of your guard to watch at all gates. Let them take away the goods and tell the men who are carrying them out of the city that they must first pay a tithe to Zeus. In doing so, you will not be hated by them for taking their goods by force, and they will willingly give up their goods, acknowledging within themselves that you are acting justly. " 90 Although Loxias eagerly desired that the calamity of Sardis might come upon the sons of Croesus rather than Croesus himself, it was not possible for him to divert the Destinies from their course. However, he did grant them a delay, putting off the taking of Sardis by three years. This allowed Croesus to be assured that he would be taken prisoner later in life than the originally predicted time. Furthermore, Loxias assisted Croesus when he was about to be burned. 93

They say these were invented at the same time as they colonized Tyrsenia. In the reign of Atys, son of Manes, their king, a severe famine struck Lydia. The Lydians endured it for a while but eventually sought remedies when it persisted. One person proposed one solution, and another suggested an alternative. As the evil did not slacken but pressed upon them ever more, their king divided the Lydian people into two parts. He appointed one part by lot to remain in the land, while the other part was allowed to depart from it. The king assigned himself to lead the part that would stay, and his son, Tyrsenos, was tasked with leading those who were departing. The Lydians were then made subject to the Persians. After this, our history proceeds to inquire about Cyrus, who was the king that destroyed the empire of Croesus, and about the Persians, in what manner they obtained control over Asia.

These having fought for their freedom with the Assyrians proved themselves good men, and thus they pushed off the yoke of slavery from themselves and were set free. The other nations also followed suit, including the Medes. When all on the continent had gained independence, they eventually returned to despotic rule. This Deïokes, having formed a desire for despotic power, did thus: Whereas the Medes dwelt in separate villages, he, being even before that time renowned in his own village, set himself to practice just dealing more thoroughly and with greater zeal than before. He did this despite much lawlessness throughout Media, and despite knowing that injustice is ever at odds with justice. Then, as more people came to him continually, because men learned that his decisions were true, Deïokes realized that everything referred back to himself. He stopped sitting in the place where he used to sit in public to hear cases, and said he would no longer hear cases, for it was not profitable for him to neglect his own affairs and attend to the cases of his neighbors all day.

After ruling over these two powerful nations, he went on to conquer Asia, moving from one nation to another until he finally marched against the Assyrians, who resided in Nineveh. The Assyrians, once rulers of the entire region, had lost their support as their allies defected from them. Despite this, they remained prosperous within their own borders. Phraortes marched against them, and was killed along with most of his army. He had ruled for 22 years before his death. 103 This king was said to have been even more warlike than his forefathers. He was the first to separate the men of Asia into distinct groups, dividing them into spearmen, archers, and horsemen. Prior to this, they had fought together without any clear distinctions between their roles. He was the one who fought alongside the Lydians when day turned into night as they clashed, and who subsequently unified all of Asia under his rule above the River Halys. He had assembled all his troops and set out on the march to Nineveh to avenge his father's death and claim the city for himself.

When he had fought a battle against the Assyrians and emerged victorious, he sat down before Nineveh. Suddenly, a large army of Scythians appeared, led by Madyas, the son of Protohyas, the Scythian king. The Persians had invaded Asia after driving the Kimmerians out of Europe, and in their pursuit as they fled, they came to the land of Media. 104 The Scythians did not invade via this route; instead, they took the upper road, 118, which was much longer. They followed this path while keeping Mount Caucasus on their right-hand side.

After Kyaxares' death, he had ruled for forty years, including those years when the Scythians held power. His son Astyages then inherited the kingdom from him. And when Mandane had reached an age suitable for marriage, she was not given to any of the Medes who were her peers, as he feared the vision. Instead, he married her to a Persian named Cambyses, who came from a noble lineage and possessed a calm demeanor, ranking him significantly lower than a Mede of middle rank. 108 Astryages then desired to protect himself from this when Cyrus was born. He called Harpagos, a man close to him whom he trusted above all other Medes, and had appointed him manager of his affairs. Astryages said to him: "Do not neglect the task I am about to assign you, Harpagos. Be cautious lest you replace me and instead of gaining an advantage for yourself, bring your own downfall."

Take the child born to Mandane and bring it to your home. Kill it there, then bury it as you wish. To the king, I replied: "Your Majesty, I have never committed an offense against you in the past. I am vigilant about my actions now and will continue to be so, lest I make a mistake in the future." She said to him: "What now will you do?" "Not according to what Astyages had ordered," he replied. "Even if he were to become even more deranged and irrational than he already is, I would not agree with his wishes or serve him in such a heinous act." I can't correct the text as it appears to be a passage from ancient Greek literature, likely Herodotus' Histories. The original text has been preserved in its original form, and any changes would alter its historical significance. However, I can suggest a corrected version of the text with improved readability:

"For many reasons I will not kill the child; first because he is related to me, and then because Astyages is old and childless. If power comes to me after his death, does it not pose the greatest danger to me?"

When the herdsman arrived, summoned by great urgency, Harpagos spoke thus: "Astyages commands you to take this child and leave it in the most desolate part of the mountains, that it may perish as soon as possible." He said to his wife: "When I arrived in the city, I encountered things that I wished I hadn't seen, and things that I'm grateful I didn't experience."

She found it. He said he couldn't do otherwise, for messengers would be constantly sent by Harpagus to ensure this was done. If he failed, he'd face a terrible fate. She couldn't convince her husband in the end. The next woman spoke up and said, "Since then, I've been unable to persuade you to show it. If it's unavoidable that people see it, do this: follow my instructions."

If I am worthy to suffer any evil, then I shall endure. " 116 As Astyages listened, he felt an unmistakable sense of recognition. The boy's features bore a striking resemblance to his own, and his response seemed too bold for one of his years. It was as if the timing of the prophecy matched the boy's age perfectly. As he gazed in awe at these wonders, he remained silent for a moment before regaining his composure. "Artembares," he said, "I intend to arrange matters so that you and your son will have no reason to complain." With this, Artembares was dismissed, and the servants led Cyrus into Astyages' presence alone.

The herdsman said he was Astyages' son, and his mother still lived with him as his wife. However, Astyages claimed the herdsman was not well-advised to seek extreme necessity. As he spoke, he signaled his guards to apprehend him. I then acted accordingly: I summoned the herdsman and handed over the child to him, stating that you were the one who ordered its death—and in this instance, I told the truth, as you had indeed commanded it.

Consider this a fortunate change of circumstances. Send your son to join the young boy who has recently arrived, and then come yourself to dine with me as a gesture of gratitude to the gods I intend to honor for preserving the boy's life. " 119 When it seemed that Harpagos was satisfied with food, Astyages asked if he had been pleased with the banquet. Harpagos replied that he had been very greatly pleased. They then brought to him the head of his son, covered in a cloth, along with his hands and feet. As they stood nearby, they instructed Harpagos to uncover the head and select whatever he desired from it. When Harpagos obeyed and uncovered, he saw the remains of his son. He did not feel overwhelmed by amazement but controlled himself. Astyages asked if he had noticed what animal he had been eating the flesh from. Harpagos replied that he had perceived it, and whatever the king did was pleasing to him.

He said he had been raised by the herdsman's wife, and praised her highly, making him the main figure in his story. As Cyrus matured into a man, he became the most courageous and beloved among his peers. Harpagos, seeking to form a friendship with him, showered Cyrus with gifts, driven by a desire for revenge against Astyages. The gods watch over you, son of Cambyses, for if they did not, you would not have achieved such great success.

Do you therefore take vengeance on Astyages, who is your murderer? For as long as his will concerns you, you are dead; yet by the care of the gods and my own efforts, you remain alive. This, I believe, you have already learned from the beginning to the end - both how it happened to you and what sufferings I endured at the hands of Astyages because I spared your life and gave you to the herdsman. If you wish to follow my guidance, you will become the ruler of all the lands under the rule of King Astyages. Now consider that everything is in order. Then take action immediately. " 125

Cyrus, having gathered all the flocks of goats and sheep and the herds of cattle from his father's estate, slaughtered them and prepared them for the entertainment of the Persian guests. He also provided wine and other suitable refreshments. The difference between them was great. The previous day brought only evil, while the current day brought only good. Now, therefore, do as I say and make yourselves free. For I believe I was born by fortunate circumstance to handle this matter, and I think you are no worse than the Medes in other respects or in those related to war.

When the Medes marched out and began fighting the Persians, some joined the battle, including those who hadn't been involved in the plan, while others defected to the Persians. The majority, however, remained inactive and fled. 128 When the Median army was shamefully defeated, Astyages immediately learned of its defeat and threatened Cyrus, saying, "Even if you are spared, you will still face punishment." Thus, having spoken, he first impaled the Magian interpreters of dreams who had persuaded him to set Cyrus free. Then, he armed the remaining Medes, both young and old men, who were left behind in the city. Astyages declared him both the most unskilful and the most unjust of men. He was considered unskilful because, when given the opportunity to become king, he had instead conferred the chief power on another. This act made him the most unjust, as it was directly related to the dinner that led to the subjugation of the Medes.

Astyages, having been king for 35 years, was thus caused to cease from being king; and the Medes stooped under the yoke of the Persians because of his cruelty. They had ruled Asia above the river Halys for 128 years, except during a period when the Scythians held rule. Afterwards, however, they realized their mistake and turned against Darius. They rebelled against him and were subsequently defeated in battle. Cyrus became king, born and bred in that land. He then conquered Croesus, who had started the conflict, as I mentioned earlier. After defeating him, Cyrus ruled over all of Asia. 131 The Persians' customs, as I understand them, are as follows: They do not build images, temples, or altars, and consider those who do so to be foolish. This, it seems to me, is because they believe gods are not like humans, unlike the Greeks. Now this is the manner of sacrifice for the gods as described by the Persians: they do not build altars or kindle fire. When they wish to make an offering, they use no libations, music from the pipe, or sprinkling meal; but when a man wishes to offer to any one of the gods, he leads the animal to a pure and unpolluted place and calls upon the god, having his tiara wreathed with a branch of myrtle.

The sacrificer then removes the flesh after a brief interval and disposes of it as desired. 133 The Persians claim that the Greeks finish their meals early, as they only serve a few solid dishes before dessert, which is not part of a single course. This leads them to believe that the Greeks leave for dinner hungry, because after dinner, there's nothing substantial served as dessert. However, if a good dessert were presented, they would likely continue eating. It is considered a mark of masculine virtue next to bravery in battle, being able to produce many offspring. The king rewards those who have borne him the most children with gifts each year, as they are seen as a source of power and strength.

They educate their children, starting at five years old, until they are twenty, in three things only: riding, shooting, and speaking the truth. However, before a boy reaches five years old, he does not meet his father but lives with the women. This is done for a reason: if the child were to die during their upbringing, it would prevent any grief from affecting their father. 137 I commend this custom of theirs, as well as the one next to be mentioned. Neither the king himself nor any other Persian shall put anyone to death for a single cause alone. Nor shall they inflict harm that is irreparable to any of their own servants. However, if after weighing the wrongs done and comparing them to the services rendered, he finds that the latter are greater, then only will he express his anger. From my own knowledge, I can say for certain that the following account of their burial practices is shrouded in secrecy. It is reported that the bodies of Persians are not buried until they have been ravaged by birds or dogs. The magicians I know for certain have this practice, and they do it openly. The Persians covered the body with wax and then buried it in the earth.

He said to them in anger, and when the Ionians heard this answer, they returned to their cities and built walls around each one separately. They then gathered at the Panionion, except for the men of Miletos, because Cyrus had sworn an agreement with only these men on the same terms as the Lydians had granted. Some were ruled by Lykian kings, descendants of Glaucos and Hippolochos. Others were ruled by Cauconians of Pylos, descendants of Codros, the son of Melanthos. Since however these hold on to the name more than the other Ionians, let them be called, if they will, the Ionians of truly pure descent. In fact, all who claim Ionian ancestry from Athens and observe the festival of Apaturia are indeed Ionians. This applies to everyone except for the people of Ephesus and Colophon, as they alone among the Ionians do not participate in the Apaturia festival, due to a murder committed by them. 148

The Aiolians lost Smyrna in the following manner: Certain men from Colophon, who had been defeated in party strife and forced to flee their homeland, sought refuge there. After this, the Colophonian exiles waited for an opportune moment when the people of Smyrna were celebrating a festival to Dionysus outside the city walls, and then they sealed the gates, gaining control of the city. When the entire Aiolian force arrived to aid them, the Ionians agreed to surrender their movable assets in exchange for the Aiolians' withdrawal from Smyrna. The Lacedemonians, however, would not listen to him; instead, they decided not to assist the Ionians.

After this, he entrusted Sardis to Tabalos, a Persian. He also gave both the gold of Croesus and that of the other Lydians to Pactyas, a Lydian, to take charge of. Then, he marched away to Agbatana, taking Croesus with him, and made no account of the Ionians at present. For Babylon stood in his way, along with the Bactrian nation, the Sacans, and the Egyptians. He intended to launch expeditions against these groups himself, while dispatching another commander to deal with the Ionians. 154 Cyrus heard this on his way and turned to Croesus and said, "What will be the outcome of these events that are unfolding?"

After this, Mazares sent messengers to Kyme, instructing them to surrender Pactyas. The men of Kyme decided to consult the god at Branchidai on how to proceed. Upon this temple, with deliberate purpose, Aristodicos went around destroying the nests of sparrows 161 and all other birds that had hatched there. As he did so, a voice came from within the inner shrine, saying: "You most impious man, why are you doing this?" You are carrying away forcibly those who seek refuge at my temple. And Aristodicos replied, "Lord, do you come to assist your suppliants, only to bid the men of Kyme surrender their goods?" The god replied, "I command you to do this, so that you will suffer even greater punishment for your impiety. This way, you will not have to return to me in the future to seek refuge for those who are seeking protection." " 160

Those of Mytilene, however, were preparing to deliver up Pactyas for a price when Mazares sent messages to them. The bargain was never completed, so I cannot say for certain what the price was. The men of Kyme learned that this was being done by the Mytilenians and sent a vessel to Lesbos, where Pactyas was conveyed to Chios. After that, he was dragged forcibly from the temple of Athena Polias by the Chians and handed over to them. The Chians then delivered him into the custody of Atarneus in exchange, who was now a region of Mysia, located on the island of Lesbos. A long time passed during which neither the Chians nor anyone else in the region of Atarneus used barley meal grown locally for sacrificial purposes or baked cakes made from the corn that grew there. Instead, all the crops from this land were barred from participating in any sacred rituals. 161

The men of Chios had then delivered up Pactyas. After this, Mazares made an expedition against those who had joined in besieging Tabalos. He first reduced to slavery those of Priene, then overran the entire plain of the Maiander, making spoil for his army. Similarly, he also conquered Magnesia. Shortly after falling ill and dying, 162 Harpagos descended after his death to assume command, as he was also a Mede by birth. This was the man whom King Astyages of the Medes had invited to an unlawful feast, and it was he who assisted in giving the kingdom to Cyrus. With this man, I say, the Phocaians became so exceedingly friendly that he persuaded them to leave Ionia and settle wherever they desired in his own land. However, when he failed to convince them, he later heard from them about the Mede's growing power and gave them money to build a wall around their city. He spared no expense, as the wall's circumference was extensive, stretching many furlongs 163 in length, built with large stones fitted together closely. 164

Harpagos said that he knew very well what they were trying to do. Nevertheless, he was willing to let them deliberate. The Phocaians, however, were unable to purchase the islands of Oinussai due to the reluctance of the men of Chios. Fearing that these islands might become a hub for trade and potentially exclude their own island from commerce, they decided to travel to Kyrnos. This was twenty years after Alalia had been established in Kyrnos, which had been founded according to an oracle. When setting out for Kyrnos, they first sailed to Phocaia. There, they killed the Persian garrison, which Harpagos had previously surrendered to the city. After this success, they made solemn vows to anyone who might be left behind on their journey. They also sank a large iron mass into the sea and swore that they would not return to Phocaia until that mass reappeared on the surface again. As they set sail for Kyrnos, more than half of the citizens felt a deep longing for their homeland and city, renouncing their oath and turning back to Phocaia.

After this, the men of Agylla discovered that everything that passed by the spot where the Phocaians were killed after being stoned became either distorted, crippled, or paralyzed. This affected both small cattle and beasts of burden as well as human creatures. In response, the men of Agylla sent a delegation to Delphi to seek purification from their offense. The Pythian prophetess advised them to follow the same ritual they still continue to perform: making great sacrifices in honor of the dead and holding an athletic contest and horse racing at the site. The Phocaeans had a fate similar to mine, as I mentioned earlier. However, those who sought refuge in Rhegion set out from there and captured the city in Oenotria, which is now known as Hyele. These Ionians alone left their native cities because they could not endure subjection. The other Ionians, except the Milesians, fought alongside Harpagos in arms like those who left their homes, proving themselves brave men as each fought for his own city. However, when they were defeated and captured, they remained in their own places and fulfilled their obligations. In contrast, the Milesians had made a sworn agreement with Cyrus himself and kept it. He urged the Ionians to set forth on a single common expedition and sail to Sardinia. Afterward, they should find a single city for all the Ionians. This would allow them to escape subjection and be prosperous, inhabiting the largest of all islands and ruling over others. In contrast, if they remained in Ionia, he did not believe that freedom would exist for them anymore.

Thales, a man of Miletos and Phoenician descent, gave sound counsel to the Ionians before their downfall. Bias of Priene later offered wise advice after Ionia's ruin. He advised the Ionians to have a single seat of government. This should be located at Teos, which was in the centre of Ionia. The other cities should continue to be inhabited as before, with equal status. These men sought counsel from me on matters of great importance. Harpagos, after subduing Ionia, marched against the Carians, Caunians, and Lykians, accompanied by Ionians and Aiolians.

The Dorians and Ionians then drove the Carians off the islands and onto the mainland. The Lykians originated from Crete, as the entire island was once inhabited by Barbarians. When the sons of Europa, including Sarpedon and Minos, arrived in Crete to dispute the kingdom, Minos emerged victorious in the conflict and expelled both Sarpedon himself and his followers. The displaced group then settled in the land of Milyas in Asia Minor, which was previously known as Milyas. The region was later inhabited by the Solymoi people. While Sarpedon ruled over them, they retained their original name, which was also used by the neighboring tribes, known as Termilai. However, when Lycos, son of Pandion from Athens, arrived in the land of the Termilai and met with Sarpedon, he too had been forced to flee by his brother Aigeus. As a result, they were later referred to as Lykians, named after Lycos.

While the Cnidians worked on it with a large number of men, it became apparent that the men who labored suffered injuries more than might have been anticipated. In fact, their afflictions seemed almost supernatural, affecting not only their bodies but particularly their eyes, as the rock was being broken up. To understand this phenomenon, the Cnidians sent envoys to consult the Oracle at Delphi about the cause of the trouble. Harpagos was conquering the coastal regions of Asia. Meanwhile, Cyrus was also expanding his empire into the upper parts of Asia, subduing every nation without exception. Babylon was the most famous and strongest city in Assyria, and it was here that the seat of their monarchy was established after Nineveh was destroyed. It lies in a great plain. The entire area measures 120 furlongs on each side, forming a square shape. As a result, the total length of the city's circuit is 480 furlongs.

As they dug the trench, they made bricks from the excavated earth. Once enough bricks were molded, they baked them in kilns. Next, using hot asphalt for mortar, they inserted reed mats at every thirty courses of brickwork. They built up the edges of the trench first and then the wall itself in the same manner. Along the top of the wall, facing each other, they constructed chambers on one story level. Between the rows of chambers, they left space to accommodate a four-horse chariot. Darius, the son of Hystaspes, designed this statue, but he did not dare to build it. Instead, his son Xerxes took up the project and had the priest killed when he tried to interfere with the statue's construction. The queen who lived after her time, named Nitocris, was wiser than the previous ruler. She left behind monuments, which I will describe. Additionally, recognizing the strength and stability of the Mede monarchy, which had already conquered Nineveh among other cities, she took steps to prepare for its continued expansion.

She made the river have a winding course and created a place dug out into a swamp, so the river would run more slowly, its force broken by going round many bends. This allowed for winding voyages to Babylon, followed by a long circuit around the pool. The body would have obstructed his view if he had driven through. He then said, "I opened the tomb and found it was empty, except for a corpse. The inscription on the body read: 'If you had not been consumed by greed and selfishness, you would not have disturbed the rest of the dead.'" " 188

The queen was said to be like what I have described. She was the mother of her son, who bore the same name as his father, Labynetos, and ruled over the Assyrians, against whom Cyrus was marching. After this threat, he ceased from his march against Babylon and divided his army into two parts. He then stretched lines and marked out straight channels, one hundred and eighty on each bank of the Gyndes, in all directions. Having disposed his army along these channels, he commanded them to dig. As a great multitude worked, the work was completed, but they spent the entire summer season at this spot working. 190 While the siege continued, Cyrus found himself at a loss for what to do, as time passed and little progress was made on his affairs. 191

Therefore, whether it was another man who suggested it to him when he was in a difficult situation, or whether he himself perceived what he ought to do, he did as follows: The main body of his army was posted at the place where the river meets the city, and then again behind the city, he set others. He proclaimed to his army that so soon as they saw that the stream had become passable, they should enter by this way into the city. The leaves of wheat and barley grow to be full four fingers broad. As for millet and sesame seeds, I can attest to their size, but I shall refrain from recording it, as I am well aware that even what has already been said about the crops produced is enough to cause disbelief in those who have not visited the Babylonian land. The boat is steered by two rowers, one on either side, with each man pulling their oar in unison. The man at the bow pushes against the water to stabilize the vessel, while the man at the stern pulls his oar to propel it forward. These vessels were made in various sizes. The largest ones had a capacity of up to five thousand talents' worth of cargo. In each vessel, there was a single live ass, while the larger ones had multiple asses.

They wear boats; and their attire consists of a linen tunic that reaches down to their feet. Over this, they wear a woolen garment, followed by a white mantle draped around them. They also wear shoes in a style reminiscent of ancient Boeotian footwear. In every village, once a year, it followed this ritual: When the maidens reached the age for marriage, they gathered together and brought them to one place. A group of men stood around them, and the crier announced each maiden's presence, selling her first to the most attractive one, then moving on to the next after she had been sold and fetched a substantial amount of money. All the wealthy men of Babylon sought to marry the most beautiful maidens. Those from lower social classes, who were also eager to wed, did not need physical beauty; instead, they accepted financial compensation and less attractive women.

This was their most honorable custom; it no longer exists today, but they have found a new way to protect themselves from being mistreated or sold into slavery. Since they were conquered, they have been oppressed and ruined. Each common person who is in need of a living has forced his female child into prostitution. 197 The Araxes is said to be larger by some and smaller by others than the Ister. Some claim that there are many islands within it, roughly equal in size to Lesbos, inhabited by people who sustain themselves during the summer by harvesting roots of various kinds they dig up, as well as certain fruits from trees they have discovered for sustenance. These fruits are stored during the season when they ripen and consumed during the winter months. It is said that other trees have been discovered, which yield a fruit of a kind. When these trees gather in groups in the same place and light a fire, they sit around it in a circle and throw some of the fruit into the flames. As the fruit burns, they smell its aroma and become intoxicated, much like the ancient Greeks with wine. The more fruit is added to the fire, the more they become intoxicated until they rise up to dance and begin to sing.

This is their manner of living. The river Araxes originates from the land of the Matienians. It then flows through the Gyndes, which Cyrus divided into 360 channels. The river discharges itself via 40 branches, all except one of which empty into swamps and shallow pools. According to local legend, men inhabit these areas who feed on raw fish and wear seal skins as clothing. The remaining branch of the Araxes flows freely into the Caspian Sea. 203 The Caspian Sea lies west of the Caucasus Mountains, which form its boundary. To the east, a vast plain stretches as far as the eye can see. The ruler of the Massagetae was a woman, who became queen upon her husband's death. Her name was Tomyris.

Cyrus sent a message to her, attempting to win her hand in marriage. However, Tomyris recognized that he was not genuinely interested in marrying her, but rather in conquering the kingdom of the Massagetai. She rejected his advances. Undeterred, Cyrus decided to take a more direct approach. He marched towards the Araxes River and launched an open expedition against the Massagetai, constructing bridges made of boats to allow his army to cross the river safely. 206 While he was busy with this labor, Tomyris sent a herald and said: "O king of the Medes, stop pursuing this endeavor. You cannot know for certain whether these events will ultimately benefit you or not. Stop doing so, I say, and become king over your own people; endure to see us ruling those we rule." You will conquer and then march directly against the kingdom of Tomyris.

It is a grave injustice that Cyrus, son of Cambyses, should submit to a woman and relinquish his claim to her land. Now therefore it seems good to me that we should cross over and proceed from the crossing point as far as they go in their retreat. Our goal is to gain an advantage over them by following this plan: The Massagetai, as I am informed, are unfamiliar with Persian customs and have never experienced great luxuries. Cut up the cattle without stint and dress the meat. Then set out for these men a banquet in our camp: provide also bowls of unmixed wine and provisions of every kind. Having done this, leave behind the most worthless part of your army and let the rest begin to retreat from the camp towards the river. For if I am not mistaken in my judgment, they will fall to the feast when they see a quantity of good things, and after that it remains for us to display great deeds. " 208

When Cyrus awoke, he pondered the vision he had seen. Since it seemed of great significance, he summoned Hystaspes and took him aside. "Hystaspes," he said, "I have reason to believe your son is plotting against me and my throne. I will explain how I know this: The gods have always shown me signs of impending danger." In the night that has passed, while I slept, I saw the eldest son standing on my shoulders. He had one wing covering all of Asia and the other covering all of Europe. You should then proceed to the fastest route back to Persia and ensure that, upon my return there after subduing these territories, you present your son to me for examination. " 210

Cyrus believed that Darius was plotting against him, but in reality, the gods had foretold his fate: he would meet his demise there and his kingdom would soon pass into the hands of Darius. After this, Cyrus and the best part of the army returned to the Araxes. The Persians who were unfit for fighting had been left behind. A third part of the Massagetai army then attacked and killed those who remained of Cyrus' army, but not without resistance. When they had overcome their enemies, they set up a feast and lay down to eat and drink. Sated, they went to sleep. She then, when she heard of the events concerning the army and her son, sent a herald to Cyrus and said: "Cyrus, do not be elated by this, for it was due to the poison from the vine that you consumed, which fills your minds with madness. Just as wine intoxicates you, evil words are released into its flow. I set a trap for you with such a potion, and it was not through bravery in battle that my son fell."

Now therefore receive my word, giving you good counsel: restore to me my son and depart from this land without penalty. If you do not, I swear by the Sun, lord of the Massagetai, that I will give you your fill of blood, insatiable as you are. " 213 They do not use iron and silver, as they do not have them in their land; instead, they have gold and bronze in abundance. 216 They have no precise limit to their lifespan. When a man grows old, his family gathers and ritually slaughters him. They also kill his cattle, and then boil the flesh for a feast.

He used the first name for the Cappadocians and the second for the people of Palestine. ii They are naturally confused in the manuscript. Can I help you with anything else? They had heaped together provisions for themselves beforehand. The current would naturally run faster on the outside and consequently would tend to turn the boat around. As a result, the inside oarsman would pull his oar constantly towards himself, while the outside man would push his oar away from him. e To keep the boat straight, turn the back of the boat into the wind. Psammetichos did these things. He was given a task: to listen for the sound that the children made when they stopped crying without making any noise.

And accordingly, it came to pass; for after a space of two years had gone by, during which the shepherd continued on his way, at length, when he opened the door and entered, both the children fell before him in entreaty and uttered the word "bekos," stretching forth their hands. As for raising the children, this is what I mentioned earlier. Additionally, during my conversation with the priests of Hephaestus in Memphis, I also learned other information.

4 The Egyptians were the first to introduce appellations for the twelve gods. The Hellenes adopted this practice from them. They also built altars, images, and temples for the gods, as well as engraved figures on stones. In fact, the Egyptians demonstrated these practices through actual evidence, which they had observed in history. From Heliopolis, however, as you ascend, Egypt becomes narrower. On one side, a mountain range belonging to Arabia stretches along its edge, running northwards towards midday and the South Wind, which blows upwards without interruption, reaching the Erythraian Sea. In this range are located the stone quarries used for cutting stone for the pyramids at Memphis.

On this side, the mountain ends where I have stated, and then takes a turn back. It is at its widest point, as I was informed, that the journey spans two months, stretching from east to west. The borders that face eastward are said to produce frankincense. 9 The priests also provided me with a strong proof concerning this land. They stated that during King Moiris' reign, whenever the Nile reached a height of eight cubits or more, it would water Egypt below Memphis. It had been nine hundred years since Moiris' death when I first heard these things from the priests. However, they warned that unless the river rises to sixteen cubits or fifteen cubits, it will not overflow the land.

The Egyptians did not originate at the same time as the region known as the Delta by the Ionians. Rather, they existed from the dawn of humanity, with their land advancing over time. As a result, many remained in their original homes, while others migrated downward to lower regions. The Oracle of Ammon's response corroborates my assertion that Egypt extends as far as I have described it. This was confirmed to me after I had already developed my own understanding of the country's boundaries. For those living in Marea and Apis, cities bordering Egypt on Libya, who believed themselves to be Libyans rather than Egyptians, and were also burdened by the rules of religious service due to their desire not to be excluded from consuming beef, they sent a message to Ammon stating that they had no connection with the Egyptians, as they resided outside the Nile Delta and shared nothing with them. They requested that it be permissible for them to eat anything without distinction.

The god, however, did not permit them to do so. Instead, he said that the land which the Nile River flowed over and watered was Egypt, and it was there that those who dwelled beneath the city of Elephantine drank from its waters. Some Hellenes who sought distinction through cleverness have described this water in three different ways. Two of these accounts I consider unnecessary to discuss further, except for highlighting their nature. One account states that the Etesian Winds are responsible for the river's rise, as they prevent the Nile from flowing into the sea. The second method demonstrates greater ignorance than previously discussed. It's even more remarkable to explain: 28. The river causes these effects because it originates from the ocean, and the ocean surrounds the entire earth. 22

If one were to explain the situation concisely, all has now been stated; regardless of which region a god approaches first and stands directly above, it can be reasonably assumed that this area is most in need of water, and its natural streams and rivers have dried up the most. 25 The Sun does not send all the water of the Nile away from itself every year, but it also allows some to remain with itself.

When winter becomes milder, the Sun returns to the heavens. From this point on, it distributes its light equally among all rivers. However, during this period, the rivers flow in large quantities as rainwater mixes with them in abundance, since their region receives rainfall, causing the torrents to swell. In the summer, however, they are weak. For it is not only that the showers of rain fail them, but also they are attracted to the sun. The Nile, however, is unique among rivers, as it does not receive rainfall and is instead drawn by the Sun. As a result, its flow is significantly reduced during the winter months compared to the summer; in fact, it flows at less than half its normal capacity during this time.

As for the account given by Etearchos the Ammonian, I will only mention what is relevant here. In Egypt, I shall make my report at length, for it boasts an abundance of wonders that surpass those of any other land. Its works, too, are numerous and impressive, far exceeding what can be put into words. This is why I must elaborate further on its greatness. In this way, the beast is tested. Their appointed manner of sacrifice is as follows: They lead the sealed beast to the altar where they are sacrificing, kindle a fire, pour libations of wine over the altar so that it runs down upon the victim, and call upon the god. Then, they cut its throat and sever the head from the body. The body then of the beast they flay, but upon the head, they make many imprecations first. Those who have a market and Hellenes sojourning among them for trade carry it to the market-place and sell it. In contrast, those who do not have any Hellenes among them cast it away into the river. This is the form of imprecation they utter upon the heads, praying that if any evil befalls either themselves or the land of Egypt in general, it may come rather upon this head.

They make their sacrifice after fasting. While the offerings are being burned, they all mourn by beating themselves. When they finish, they depart as a feast, taking what was left unburned from the sacrifice. 41 The men of Thebes and those who follow their example claim that this custom originated from Heracles' desire to see Zeus. However, Zeus did not want to be seen by him. Eventually, when Heracles persisted in his entreaties, Zeus devised a plan. He flayed a ram, held the head of the ram in front of him, and then donned the fleece, allowing him to appear to Zeus. The Egyptians depict Zeus as a ram's face, followed by the Ammonians, who also adopt this image after being influenced by both Egyptian and Ethiopian settlers. They speak a language combining elements of both tongues. In my view, it is likely that the Ammonians borrowed their name from this god, as the Egyptians refer to him as Zeus Amun.

The Thebans do not sacrifice rams but hold them sacred. On one day in a particular year, on the feast of Zeus, they cut up a single ram in the same manner as before and cover its skin with an image of Zeus. They then bring up to it another image of Heracles. Heracles is not an ancient Egyptian god. He was a demigod in Greek mythology, the son of Zeus and a mortal woman, Alcmene. In fact, however, Heracles is often associated with Egypt due to the influence of the Ptolemaic dynasty, which was of Greek origin. The Ptolemies were known for their reverence for Egyptian gods and goddesses, including Heracles, who was worshipped as a god in Egypt during the Hellenistic period. It's worth noting that the dates mentioned in the original text are likely to be incorrect or misleading. The reason why Egyptians who have been mentioned earlier do not sacrifice goats, whether female or male, is that the Mendesians consider Pan one of their eight gods. According to them, these eight gods existed before the twelve gods, and the painters and sculptors depict Pan in their art with a goat's face and legs, much like the Hellenes do. However, they do not believe he was actually depicted this way but rather resembled the other gods. As for why they chose to represent him in this form, I shall refrain from discussing it further. He did not take the entire thing into account, but rather those wise men who followed him were able to convey its significance more fully. The naming of 51 of almost all the gods has come to Hellas from Egypt. For this reason, I find it true that it comes from the Barbarians. Upon inquiry, I am of opinion that most probably it comes from Egypt, because, except in the case of Poseidon and the Dioscuroi (in accordance with what I have said before), as well as Hera, Hestia, Themis, the Charites, and the Nereïds, the Egyptians have had the names of all the other gods in their country for all time. Afterwards, when much time had elapsed, they learned from Egypt the names of the gods, all except Dionysos, whose name they learned long afterwards. Later, the Pelasgians consulted the Oracle at Dodona about the names, as this prophetic site is considered the most ancient among the Hellenes' oracles, and it was the only one at that time.

The things that come first are said by the priestesses of Dodona, while the others, those concerning Hesiod and Homer, I will discuss later. 54 The priestesses of Dodona shared their knowledge with me. The eldest, Promeneia, was followed by Timarete, and then Nicandra, the youngest. The other inhabitants of Dodona, involved in the temple's affairs, corroborated their stories. 56

The women were called doves by the people of Dodona because they were perceived as barbarians and their voices sounded like those of birds. However, after some time, it is said that the dove spoke with a human voice, which is when the woman began to communicate in a way that could be understood. As long as she spoke in a barbarian tongue, her voice still seemed to resemble that of a bird. If the dove had truly been a bird, how could it have spoken with a human voice? They celebrate a festival in every city along the riverbank. The lamps are saucers filled with salt and oil mixed, and the wick floats freely on the surface, burning throughout the night. This festival is known as Lychnocaia, or the lighting of the lamps.

Cats are taken to sacred buildings in the city of Bubastis when they die, where they are embalmed before being buried. The bears, which are rarely seen, and the wolves, not much larger in size than foxes, bury them on the spot where they are found lying. 68 The nature of a crocodile is as follows: during the four coldest months, this creature eats nothing. It has four feet and belongs to both land and water. On land, it produces eggs that hatch, and for most of the day, it remains on dry ground. However, at night, it spends its time in the river, where the water is warmer than the open air and dew.

Sacred animals have been a subject of discussion and reverence throughout history. 77 Birds eat quails, ducks, and small birds raw, after first curing them. They also consume all other animals that belong to the bird or fish classes, excluding those considered sacred, which are either roasted or boiled. 78 The ancient Greeks sang a song in honor of Linus, and I am curious to know how it relates to the Egyptian myth. They are not in agreement with any of the Hellenes; instead of addressing one another on the roads, they show reverence, lowering their hands to their knees. 81 They wear tunics of linen about their legs with fringes, which they call calasiris. Above these, they have garments of white wool draped over them. People have discovered many portents beyond others. When a portent occurs, they observe and record the outcome. The third kind of embalming, by which bodies are prepared for those who have limited means, involves cleansing the abdomen with a purgative. People who eat only fish live on them. They catch the fish, remove the guts, and let them dry in the sun before eating them. 93

Fish that swim in schools are not much produced in rivers, but are bred in lakes. When they feel the urge to breed, they swim out in schools towards the sea. The males lead the way, shedding their sperm as they go, while the females follow and swallow it up, becoming impregnated. Once they have become full of young in the sea, each school returns to its own haunts. The same principle no longer applies, but the females now take the lead. They follow the same pattern as before, laying eggs in small clusters, about 79 at a time. The males then follow, consuming the eggs one by one. These grains are fish. The surviving grains give rise to new fish, which are then bred up.

They tend to stay near the shore because when swimming towards the sea, they remain on the left side of the river, and when swimming back upstream, they also stick to the same side, getting as close to the bank as possible, in order to avoid straying from their path due to the stream. These boats cannot sail up the river unless there is a very fresh wind blowing. Instead, they are towed from the shore. Downstream, however, their journey proceeds as follows: The boat has a door-shaped crate made of tamarisk wood and reed mats sewn together. Additionally, it carries a stone weighing about two talents, which has been bored with a hole. The boatman then allows the crate to float in front of the boat, securing it with a rope, while dragging the stone behind by another rope. As the force of the stream presses upon it, the crate moves swiftly along the baris, a type of boat. Behind it, a stone is dragged through the water, keeping its course straight despite being submerged.

As they dined, she allowed the river to flood their table by a hidden channel. As for the other kings, they told me of no great works produced by them. The king who succeeded them was Sesostris. When I considered the matter, I asked both groups about their knowledge. The Colchians remembered the Egyptians better than the Egyptians did about the Colchians. However, the Egyptians claimed to believe that the Colchians were part of Sesostris' army. The Egyptians and Ethiopians are the only two groups I can identify as having practiced circumcision. However, it is unclear which group learned from the other. It is evident that this custom is very old. Nevertheless, it is clear that other nations adopted circumcision through contact with the Egyptians. For example, the Phoenicians, who have trade relations with Greece, no longer follow Egyptian customs in this regard and do not circumcise their children. 105

The Hellenes, however, thought they were being mocked by them and besieged the city until they finally took it. The priest, blindfolded, was led into the temple of Demeter, located twenty furlongs outside the city. Afterward, two wolves guided him back to the starting point. 123

During the reign of Rhampsinitos, it was said that Egypt experienced a period of orderly rule and great prosperity. However, after his death, Cheops became king and brought about 104 years of misfortune to the Egyptians. He closed all the temples, first preventing them from performing sacrifices there, and then ordered all Egyptians to work for him. Some were appointed to transport stones from the Arabian mountains to the Nile, while others were ordered to receive the stones after they had been carried over the river in boats and then transported to the Libyan mountains. A large workforce of 100,000 men worked continuously for three months at a time. After ten years of oppression, they built a causeway by drawing stones. The length of this causeway is five furlongs, while its breadth is ten fathoms. At its highest point, the height reaches eight fathoms. The causeway is made of stone, with figures carved upon it.

For this, they said, the ten years were spent. He had built underground chambers on the hill upon which the pyramids stood, which he caused to be made as sepulchral chambers for himself in an island. Having conducted a channel from the Nile there. The highest parts of it were finished first, and afterwards they proceeded to finish what came next to them. Finally, they completed the lower sections nearest the ground. The Egyptian pharaoh Cheops ruled for 50 years. His brother, Chephren, took over the throne after his death. They said that Chephren reigned for 56 years. 128
